Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.

These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).

The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|lt=borehole drilling}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.
== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺}}

{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} {{afc comment |1=unsure about the translation of the name. I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|lt=borehole drilling}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.

These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}

Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺}}

{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} {{afc comment |1=unsure about the translation of the name. I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺}}

Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺}}

{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} {{afc comment |1=unsure about the translation of the name. I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺}}

However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺}}

I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社}}.

These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉}}

== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling|12=draft}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=draft}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳|12=draft}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=draft}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=draft}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=draft}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖|12=draft}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=draft}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=draft}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=draft}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=draft}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=draft}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=draft}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=draft}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|ボーリング|" to "|ボーリング|lt=Borehole drilling|1=Boring (earth)|2=ja|3=ボーリング|4=fr|5=Forage|6=zh|7=钻井|8=pl|9=Wiertnictwo|"
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} {{afc comment |1=unsure about the translation of the name. I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|12=draft}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|lt=Borehole drilling|1=Boring (earth)|2=ja|3=ボーリング|4=fr|5=Forage|6=zh|7=钻井|8=pl|9=Wiertnictwo|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling|12=draft}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=draft}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳|12=draft}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=draft}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=draft}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=draft}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖|12=draft}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=draft}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=draft}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=draft}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=draft}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=draft}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=draft}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=draft}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2018年11月}} '''開湯伝説'''（かいとうでんせつ）とは、[[温泉]]が発見された由来に関する言い伝え･伝説。あくまで言い伝えであり、史実とは異なる（後述）。
== 概要 == その内容は温泉地により異なり、また温泉によっては複数の伝説が存在する温泉もある。必ずしも全ての温泉に存在するものではなく、主に歴史が古い温泉に開湯伝説が存在する。
由来としては、「[[神話]]に基づくもの」、「[[僧侶]]・[[武将]]による発見」、「[[動物]]が発見したもの」などがある。このような開湯伝説が創られる理由として、'''口頭説明に信憑性をもたらすため'''だとしている。すなわち、口先で[[旅人]]が語っても、なかなか相手に信じてもらえないが、それを敢えて鳥獣が[[傷]]を癒しているの見た、あるいは著名な武将や高僧、[[記紀]]の[[英雄]]らが発見したと[[フィクション]]を加えることで、相手をそのように思いこませる説得手段であるという。つまるところ、開湯伝説の大半はフィクションであり、実際の発見者は近郷の農民、杣人などが多い。
その開湯伝説の目的は、いわゆる「おらが湯」の宣伝活動に因るところが大きい。今日でも各温泉地において、この開湯伝説が盛んに喧伝されているのはこのためであり、この傾向は[[江戸時代]]やそれ以前から見られた。
したがって、情報化が進んだ今日のように[[ボーリング]]で開発される温泉では、開湯伝説が存在することは稀である。今日の場合、例えば[[吉井温泉 (福岡県)|吉井温泉]]のように温泉開発者が神のお告げを夢で見た、など開発者の思いによるものがわずかに存在する程度である。
== 神話および皇族によるもの ==
各地に[[大国主命]]と[[少彦名命]]の開湯伝説がある。これら神話上の人物による開湯は仏教伝来以前の古湯に見られるケースで、彼らが開湯の主役に祭り上げられる理由は、[[先祖]]、英雄、開拓者などの根強い崇拝のためである。また、歴史の真偽は別問題として、神話の人物は、一豪族の長など有力者が多く、医薬に通じていた者も多かったとされる。たとえば、大国主命は医薬に対する知識は明るかったとされ、それは[[因幡の白兎]]伝説の際の[[火傷]]治療などでその高い知識が垣間見られる。また、[[少彦名命]]も医薬に精しい神として知られ、温泉信仰の拠り処となっている。
*[[道後温泉]] - 湯に浸かって平癒した少彦名命が踊り、その足跡が記されているという石（玉石）が遺されている。大国主命が手のひらに少彦名命を載せた模様を掘り込んだ湯釜が[[道後温泉本館]]の浴槽に設置されている。『伊予国風土記』逸文には、この時、大国主命が大分の[[鶴見岳]]の山麓から湧く「[[速見の湯]]」（現在の[[別府温泉]]）を海底に管を通して道後温泉へと導いたという[[神話]]が記載されている。 *[[有馬温泉]] - 大己貴命と少彦名命が、湯を浴び傷を癒す三羽のカラスの様子から発見したとされる。 *[[奥津温泉]] - 西日本各地に皇族が来湯した伝説が残っている。 *[[嬉野温泉]] - 神功皇后 *[[武雄温泉]] - 神功皇后 *[[南紀白浜温泉]] - 持統天皇他、[[日本書紀]]による
== 人物による発見伝説 == 歴史上の人物による発見は[[行基]]、[[空海]]（弘法大師）、[[一遍]]などの僧侶による発見などの言い伝えが各地に残っている。これは、[[仏教]]においては病を退けて福を招来するものとして入浴が奨励され、貧しい人々や病人・囚人らを対象として[[施浴]]がおこなわれていた為である。行基が発見したものは北陸をはじめ、各地に多い。東北地方では、[[坂上田村麻呂]]による発見伝説が多く残されている。また[[役行者]]らの山伏による発見伝説も多い。
著名な人物による発見伝説で共通することは、開湯伝説を作成する際に名前を引用しただけの場合が多く存在することである。特に、東北地方の[[坂上田村麻呂]]にまつわる開湯伝説はその大半が名前を借りただけである。また弘法大師による開湯伝説の場合、[[高野聖]]のうち、いわゆる山師的なものが温泉を探り当てた際に教祖たる空海の名を借用したともいわれる。
また、仏教の教えは人身の健康にも通じていた。それゆえ、僧侶は医薬にも精通していた者が多く、湯治の場として温泉を勧めた僧もいたという。そして、温泉により傷や病が癒えたことで、御利益があったと見做すことになり、温泉信仰が根付くようになった。つまり、信仰色が強い湯治場などで、僧侶による発見伝も多く、その効能や効果を世に広く謳うために、[[温泉療養]]に関連性の強い僧侶、とりわけその中で高名な人物を引き合いに出したという具合である。
高名な人物を借用するのは、前述の通り先賢の崇拝という部分が強いが、[[鎌倉仏教]]が広がる中で、それまで漠然として信仰の存在となっていた温泉に対し、医学的な活用がウェイトを占め、実用的、実益的なものになる。よって、[[鎌倉時代]]以降の人物が発見したケースでは史実に基づいたものも多い。たとえば、鎌倉中期には[[大友頼泰]]が温泉奉行を置いて[[浜脇温泉]]、[[別府温泉]]を整備しており、その湯治場の一つ[[楠温泉]]には[[元寇]]の役の戦傷者が保養に来た記録が残っている。[[1276年]]（建治2年）には、九州各地を勧進していた[[一遍]]が別府に立ち寄り、[[火男火売神社]]の導きにより「玖倍理湯の井」を鎮めて[[鉄輪温泉]]の石風呂（現在の[[鉄輪むし湯]]）を開いたとされる。[[武田信玄]]は多くの隠し湯を持っていたとされるが、これは傷兵の平癒という実益を兼ねたものである。また、信玄自身も[[下部温泉]]で、[[川中島の戦い]]での傷を癒している。これらは、英雄崇拝という部分も見られる（他の人が発見したかも知れないが、それを恰も郷土の英雄である武将が発見したような喧伝がなされている点から）が、それ以上にこの頃になると医学の知識も向上しており、信仰の対象でもあった温泉の実用的な効能において、かなり強い信憑性を帯びていたことが挙げられる。
以下に、主要な人物とそれが発見したとされる温泉の例を挙げる。
=== 僧侶 === ==== 行基 ==== {{columns-list|colwidth=20em| * [[作並温泉]]（宮城県） * [[東山温泉]]（福島県） * [[芦ノ牧温泉]]（福島県） * [[草津温泉]]（群馬県） * [[藪塚温泉]]（群馬県） * [[野沢温泉]]（長野県） * [[渋温泉]]（長野県） * [[湯田中温泉]]（長野県） * [[山代温泉]]（石川県） * [[雲仙温泉]]（長崎県） * [[山中温泉]]（石川県） * [[吉奈温泉]] * [[谷津温泉]] * [[蓮台寺温泉]]（静岡県） * [[三谷温泉]] * [[木津温泉]] * [[関金温泉]]（鳥取県） * [[塩江温泉]] }}
他にも、[[有馬温泉]]、[[湯河原温泉]]などにも行基にまつわる伝承が残っている。
==== 空海 ==== {{columns-list|colwidth=20em| * [[あつみ温泉]]（山形） * [[大塩温泉 (福島県)|大塩温泉]]（福島） * [[芦ノ牧温泉]]（福島） * [[出湯温泉]]（新潟） * [[瀬戸口温泉]]（新潟） * [[清津峡温泉]]（新潟） * [[関温泉]]（新潟） * [[燕温泉]]（新潟） * [[川場温泉]]（群馬） * [[法師温泉]]（群馬） * [[修善寺温泉]]（静岡） * [[伊豆山温泉]]（静岡） * [[信玄の湯 湯村温泉]]（山梨） * [[鹿塩温泉]]（長野） * [[海の口温泉]]（長野） * [[赤引温泉]]（愛知） * [[龍神温泉]]（和歌山） * [[関金温泉]]（鳥取） * [[湯免温泉]]（山口） * [[湯之谷温泉]]（愛媛） * [[鷹子温泉|東道後温泉]]（愛媛） * [[杖立温泉]]（熊本） * [[熊の川温泉]]（佐賀） * [[波佐見温泉]]（長崎） }}
==== 一遍 ==== * [[鉄輪温泉]]（大分） ** [[鉄輪むし湯]]
=== 武将 === ==== 坂上田村麻呂 ==== {{columns-list|colwidth=20em| * [[中房温泉]]（長野県） * 蓼科温泉（長野県） * [[梨木温泉]] * [[四万温泉]]（群馬県） * [[岳温泉]] * [[大沢温泉 (岩手県)|大沢温泉]] * [[志戸平温泉]]（岩手県） * [[台温泉]] * [[藤七温泉]] * [[男鹿温泉郷]]（秋田県） }}
== 鳥獣による発見伝説 == 鳥獣が発見したもの（正確には動物が湯に浸かっているのを見た古人が温泉であることを発見したもの）としては、「[[白|白い]]」動物にちなむものが多い。中には、「お告げ」に近い伝説もある。以下に動物とその温泉の例を挙げる。
また、動物が発見したという部分自体がフィクションの場合も存在し、その場合は前述の温泉信仰が関わっている場合が大きい（白い動物が多いのも、これに因む）。
しかし、実際は鳥獣たちが発見したのではなく、それが温泉だと発見したのはそれを目撃した村人（猟師や樵夫など）たちである。動物は本能的に[[水遊び|水浴び]]などをすることで、体の疲れを癒す習性があり、自然に湧出したいで湯などもその一環として利用されていたに過ぎない。それを敢えて、効能の説得のしやすさや親しみやすさを引き出すために、身近な野生の鳥獣を持ち上げたといえる。
また、この鳥獣が発見したという伝説では、決まって「外傷を癒した」という文句がある。胃腸病や関節痛などは不可視的なものであるために、本当に動物がそれを癒しているか分からないが、外傷なら目に見えるものであるために、誰にでも説得できるからである。もっとも、この鳥獣が発見した温泉は、開湯伝説とは裏腹に、外傷にはさほど効果を発揮せず、むしろそれ以外の部分に効果を発揮することがある。これは前述の通り、鳥獣がいで湯で癒していたのは、必ずしも外傷の治療でなく、単に疲れを癒していた本能的なもの、あるいはたまたまいで湯に鳥獣がいたという偶然として考えられるからである。
*白鷺 - [[下呂温泉]]、[[山中温泉]]、[[道後温泉]]など *コウノトリ - [[城崎温泉]]など *白キツネ - [[湯田温泉]]など *白狼 - [[三朝温泉]]など *鹿 - [[山鹿温泉]]、[[鹿教湯温泉]]など *猿 - [[俵山温泉]]など *蛇 - [[老神温泉]]
上記のなかには複数の伝説を有するものもある。
==参考文献== {{No footnotes|date=2018年11月|section=1}} * 野口冬人『全国温泉大事典』（旅行読売出版社、1997年）pp.706-710.
==関連項目== *[[温泉神社]] *[[温泉寺]]
{{onsen-stub}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2018年11月}} '''開湯伝説'''（かいとうでんせつ）とは、[[温泉]]が発見された由来に関する言い伝え･伝説。あくまで言い伝えであり、史実とは異なる（後述）。
== 概要 == その内容は温泉地により異なり、また温泉によっては複数の伝説が存在する温泉もある。必ずしも全ての温泉に存在するものではなく、主に歴史が古い温泉に開湯伝説が存在する。
由来としては、「[[神話]]に基づくもの」、「[[僧侶]]・[[武将]]による発見」、「[[動物]]が発見したもの」などがある。このような開湯伝説が創られる理由として、'''口頭説明に信憑性をもたらすため'''だとしている。すなわち、口先で[[旅人]]が語っても、なかなか相手に信じてもらえないが、それを敢えて鳥獣が[[傷]]を癒しているの見た、あるいは著名な武将や高僧、[[記紀]]の[[英雄]]らが発見したと[[フィクション]]を加えることで、相手をそのように思いこませる説得手段であるという。つまるところ、開湯伝説の大半はフィクションであり、実際の発見者は近郷の農民、杣人などが多い。
その開湯伝説の目的は、いわゆる「おらが湯」の宣伝活動に因るところが大きい。今日でも各温泉地において、この開湯伝説が盛んに喧伝されているのはこのためであり、この傾向は[[江戸時代]]やそれ以前から見られた。
したがって、情報化が進んだ今日のように[[ボーリング]]で開発される温泉では、開湯伝説が存在することは稀である。今日の場合、例えば[[吉井温泉 (福岡県)|吉井温泉]]のように温泉開発者が神のお告げを夢で見た、など開発者の思いによるものがわずかに存在する程度である。
== 神話および皇族によるもの ==
各地に[[大国主命]]と[[少彦名命]]の開湯伝説がある。これら神話上の人物による開湯は仏教伝来以前の古湯に見られるケースで、彼らが開湯の主役に祭り上げられる理由は、[[先祖]]、英雄、開拓者などの根強い崇拝のためである。また、歴史の真偽は別問題として、神話の人物は、一豪族の長など有力者が多く、医薬に通じていた者も多かったとされる。たとえば、大国主命は医薬に対する知識は明るかったとされ、それは[[因幡の白兎]]伝説の際の[[火傷]]治療などでその高い知識が垣間見られる。また、[[少彦名命]]も医薬に精しい神として知られ、温泉信仰の拠り処となっている。
*[[道後温泉]] - 湯に浸かって平癒した少彦名命が踊り、その足跡が記されているという石（玉石）が遺されている。大国主命が手のひらに少彦名命を載せた模様を掘り込んだ湯釜が[[道後温泉本館]]の浴槽に設置されている。『伊予国風土記』逸文には、この時、大国主命が大分の[[鶴見岳]]の山麓から湧く「[[速見の湯]]」（現在の[[別府温泉]]）を海底に管を通して道後温泉へと導いたという[[神話]]が記載されている。 *[[有馬温泉]] - 大己貴命と少彦名命が、湯を浴び傷を癒す三羽のカラスの様子から発見したとされる。 *[[奥津温泉]] - 西日本各地に皇族が来湯した伝説が残っている。 *[[嬉野温泉]] - 神功皇后 *[[武雄温泉]] - 神功皇后 *[[南紀白浜温泉]] - 持統天皇他、[[日本書紀]]による
== 人物による発見伝説 == 歴史上の人物による発見は[[行基]]、[[空海]]（弘法大師）、[[一遍]]などの僧侶による発見などの言い伝えが各地に残っている。これは、[[仏教]]においては病を退けて福を招来するものとして入浴が奨励され、貧しい人々や病人・囚人らを対象として[[施浴]]がおこなわれていた為である。行基が発見したものは北陸をはじめ、各地に多い。東北地方では、[[坂上田村麻呂]]による発見伝説が多く残されている。また[[役行者]]らの山伏による発見伝説も多い。
著名な人物による発見伝説で共通することは、開湯伝説を作成する際に名前を引用しただけの場合が多く存在することである。特に、東北地方の[[坂上田村麻呂]]にまつわる開湯伝説はその大半が名前を借りただけである。また弘法大師による開湯伝説の場合、[[高野聖]]のうち、いわゆる山師的なものが温泉を探り当てた際に教祖たる空海の名を借用したともいわれる。
また、仏教の教えは人身の健康にも通じていた。それゆえ、僧侶は医薬にも精通していた者が多く、湯治の場として温泉を勧めた僧もいたという。そして、温泉により傷や病が癒えたことで、御利益があったと見做すことになり、温泉信仰が根付くようになった。つまり、信仰色が強い湯治場などで、僧侶による発見伝も多く、その効能や効果を世に広く謳うために、[[温泉療養]]に関連性の強い僧侶、とりわけその中で高名な人物を引き合いに出したという具合である。
高名な人物を借用するのは、前述の通り先賢の崇拝という部分が強いが、[[鎌倉仏教]]が広がる中で、それまで漠然として信仰の存在となっていた温泉に対し、医学的な活用がウェイトを占め、実用的、実益的なものになる。よって、[[鎌倉時代]]以降の人物が発見したケースでは史実に基づいたものも多い。たとえば、鎌倉中期には[[大友頼泰]]が温泉奉行を置いて[[浜脇温泉]]、[[別府温泉]]を整備しており、その湯治場の一つ[[楠温泉]]には[[元寇]]の役の戦傷者が保養に来た記録が残っている。[[1276年]]（建治2年）には、九州各地を勧進していた[[一遍]]が別府に立ち寄り、[[火男火売神社]]の導きにより「玖倍理湯の井」を鎮めて[[鉄輪温泉]]の石風呂（現在の[[鉄輪むし湯]]）を開いたとされる。[[武田信玄]]は多くの隠し湯を持っていたとされるが、これは傷兵の平癒という実益を兼ねたものである。また、信玄自身も[[下部温泉]]で、[[川中島の戦い]]での傷を癒している。これらは、英雄崇拝という部分も見られる（他の人が発見したかも知れないが、それを恰も郷土の英雄である武将が発見したような喧伝がなされている点から）が、それ以上にこの頃になると医学の知識も向上しており、信仰の対象でもあった温泉の実用的な効能において、かなり強い信憑性を帯びていたことが挙げられる。
以下に、主要な人物とそれが発見したとされる温泉の例を挙げる。
=== 僧侶 === ==== 行基 ==== {{columns-list|colwidth=20em| * [[作並温泉]]（宮城県） * [[東山温泉]]（福島県） * [[芦ノ牧温泉]]（福島県） * [[草津温泉]]（群馬県） * [[藪塚温泉]]（群馬県） * [[野沢温泉]]（長野県） * [[渋温泉]]（長野県） * [[湯田中温泉]]（長野県） * [[山代温泉]]（石川県） * [[雲仙温泉]]（長崎県） * [[山中温泉]]（石川県） * [[吉奈温泉]] * [[谷津温泉]] * [[蓮台寺温泉]]（静岡県） * [[三谷温泉]] * [[木津温泉]] * [[関金温泉]]（鳥取県） * [[塩江温泉]] }}
他にも、[[有馬温泉]]、[[湯河原温泉]]などにも行基にまつわる伝承が残っている。
==== 空海 ==== {{columns-list|colwidth=20em| * [[あつみ温泉]]（山形） * [[大塩温泉 (福島県)|大塩温泉]]（福島） * [[芦ノ牧温泉]]（福島） * [[出湯温泉]]（新潟） * [[瀬戸口温泉]]（新潟） * [[清津峡温泉]]（新潟） * [[関温泉]]（新潟） * [[燕温泉]]（新潟） * [[川場温泉]]（群馬） * [[法師温泉]]（群馬） * [[修善寺温泉]]（静岡） * [[伊豆山温泉]]（静岡） * [[信玄の湯 湯村温泉]]（山梨） * [[鹿塩温泉]]（長野） * [[海の口温泉]]（長野） * [[赤引温泉]]（愛知） * [[龍神温泉]]（和歌山） * [[関金温泉]]（鳥取） * [[湯免温泉]]（山口） * [[湯之谷温泉]]（愛媛） * [[鷹子温泉|東道後温泉]]（愛媛） * [[杖立温泉]]（熊本） * [[熊の川温泉]]（佐賀） * [[波佐見温泉]]（長崎） }}
==== 一遍 ==== * [[鉄輪温泉]]（大分） ** [[鉄輪むし湯]]
=== 武将 === ==== 坂上田村麻呂 ==== {{columns-list|colwidth=20em| * [[中房温泉]]（長野県） * 蓼科温泉（長野県） * [[梨木温泉]] * [[四万温泉]]（群馬県） * [[岳温泉]] * [[大沢温泉 (岩手県)|大沢温泉]] * [[志戸平温泉]]（岩手県） * [[台温泉]] * [[藤七温泉]] * [[男鹿温泉郷]]（秋田県） }}
== 鳥獣による発見伝説 == 鳥獣が発見したもの（正確には動物が湯に浸かっているのを見た古人が温泉であることを発見したもの）としては、「[[白|白い]]」動物にちなむものが多い。中には、「お告げ」に近い伝説もある。以下に動物とその温泉の例を挙げる。
また、動物が発見したという部分自体がフィクションの場合も存在し、その場合は前述の温泉信仰が関わっている場合が大きい（白い動物が多いのも、これに因む）。
しかし、実際は鳥獣たちが発見したのではなく、それが温泉だと発見したのはそれを目撃した村人（猟師や樵夫など）たちである。動物は本能的に[[水遊び|水浴び]]などをすることで、体の疲れを癒す習性があり、自然に湧出したいで湯などもその一環として利用されていたに過ぎない。それを敢えて、効能の説得のしやすさや親しみやすさを引き出すために、身近な野生の鳥獣を持ち上げたといえる。
また、この鳥獣が発見したという伝説では、決まって「外傷を癒した」という文句がある。胃腸病や関節痛などは不可視的なものであるために、本当に動物がそれを癒しているか分からないが、外傷なら目に見えるものであるために、誰にでも説得できるからである。もっとも、この鳥獣が発見した温泉は、開湯伝説とは裏腹に、外傷にはさほど効果を発揮せず、むしろそれ以外の部分に効果を発揮することがある。これは前述の通り、鳥獣がいで湯で癒していたのは、必ずしも外傷の治療でなく、単に疲れを癒していた本能的なもの、あるいはたまたまいで湯に鳥獣がいたという偶然として考えられるからである。
*白鷺 - [[下呂温泉]]、[[山中温泉]]、[[道後温泉]]など *コウノトリ - [[城崎温泉]]など *白キツネ - [[湯田温泉]]など *白狼 - [[三朝温泉]]など *鹿 - [[山鹿温泉]]、[[鹿教湯温泉]]など *猿 - [[俵山温泉]]など *蛇 - [[老神温泉]]
上記のなかには複数の伝説を有するものもある。
==参考文献== {{No footnotes|date=2018年11月|section=1}} * 野口冬人『全国温泉大事典』（旅行読売出版社、1997年）pp.706-710.

{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} {{afc comment |1=unsure about the translation of the name. I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|12=draft}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|lt=Borehole drilling|1=Boring (earth)|2=ja|3=ボーリング|4=fr|5=Forage|6=zh|7=钻井|8=pl|9=Wiertnictwo|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling|12=draft}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=draft}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳|12=draft}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=draft}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=draft}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=draft}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|12=draft}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|12=draft}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖|12=draft}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=draft}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=draft}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|12=draft}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=draft}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|12=draft}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=draft}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=draft}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=draft}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=draft}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=draft}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=draft}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=draft}}

Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|qq=draft}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖|qq=draft}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|qq=draft}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|qq=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft}}

{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} {{afc comment |1=unsure about the translation of the name. I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|qq=draft}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|lt=Borehole drilling|1=Boring (earth)|2=ja|3=ボーリング|4=fr|5=Forage|6=zh|7=钻井|8=pl|9=Wiertnictwo|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling|qq=draft}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|qq=draft}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|qq=draft}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳|qq=draft}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|qq=draft}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|qq=draft}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|qq=draft}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|qq=draft}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|qq=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|qq=draft}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|qq=draft}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖|qq=draft}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|qq=draft}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|qq=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft}}

== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|qq=draft}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳|qq=draft}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|qq=draft}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|qq=draft}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|qq=draft}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|qq=draft}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|qq=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|qq=draft}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|qq=draft}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖|qq=draft}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|qq=draft}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|qq=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft}}

{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} {{afc comment |1=unsure about the translation of the name. I’m taking a bit of liberty with the title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:07, 15 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉|1=Onsen|1=Onsen|qq=draft}}. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like {{ill|boring (earth)|ja|ボーリング|lt=Borehole drilling|1=Boring (earth)|2=ja|3=ボーリング|4=fr|5=Forage|6=zh|7=钻井|8=pl|9=Wiertnictwo|1=Boring (earth)|lt=Borehole drilling|lt=borehole drilling|qq=draft}}, hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|qq=draft}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|qq=draft}}''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from {{ill|Tsurumi-dake|ja|鶴見岳|qq=draft}} in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|qq=draft}}. * '''{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|qq=draft}}''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|qq=draft}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|qq=draft}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|qq=draft}} (Kōbō Daishi), or {{ill|Ippen|ja|一遍|qq=draft}}. These stories reflect {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教|qq=draft}}'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|qq=draft}}, are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of {{ill|Kōya Hijiri|ja|高野聖|qq=draft}} seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|qq=draft}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|別府温泉|qq=draft}}. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|qq=draft}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanaka Onsen|ja|山中温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|ja|道後温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft}}
