revision.text:
{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|qq=|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|qq=|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|qq=|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|qq=|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|qq=|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|qq=|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|qq=|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|qq=|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|qq=|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.

These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|開湯伝説|version=103788656|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|開湯伝説|version=103788656|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Legends]]
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:開湯伝説]] {{Unreferenced section|date=November 2018}} '''Onsen Legends''' (開湯伝説, Kaitō Densetsu) are traditional tales and legends about the origins of the discovery of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]. These are oral traditions and differ from historical fact (explained below).
== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs. The origins often involve mythology, discoveries by monks or samurai, or animals uncovering the springs. These legends were created primarily to "lend credibility" to verbal explanations. For instance, simply telling travelers about a hot spring might not persuade them, but claiming that a mythical bird or an esteemed historical figure discovered it could add convincing narrative weight. In reality, many springs were likely discovered by local farmers or woodsmen.
The primary purpose of these legends was to promote the onsen as "our local spring." Even today, many hot spring areas actively propagate these legends as a form of advertisement, a practice that dates back to the Edo period or earlier.
In modern times, with the advent of technological development like [[:en:Boring (earth)|borehole drilling]], hot springs rarely have associated legends. However, in some cases, developers may attribute the discovery to dreams or divine messages, such as the legend of {{ill|Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|ja|吉井温泉_(福岡県)|lt=Yoshii Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshii Onsen (Fukuoka)|qq=}}.
== Mythological and Imperial Origins == Many legends feature the deities {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} as discoverers of hot springs. These figures are common in legends surrounding ancient springs from pre-Buddhist times, reflecting reverence for ancestors, heroes, and pioneers. Historically, these mythological figures may have been influential local leaders or practitioners of medicine.
For example: * '''[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]]''': The legend claims Sukunahikona danced in the healing waters, leaving his footprints behind on a stone (Tamaishi). Ōkuninushi is also said to have drawn hot water from [[:en:Mount Tsurumi|Tsurumi-dake]] in Ōita to Dōgo through an undersea conduit, as recorded in the lost text of the {{ill|Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja|伊予国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo-no-Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578975}}. * '''[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]]''': The deities Ōkuninushi and Sukunahikona discovered the spring after observing three crows bathing to heal their wounds. * '''{{ill|Okutsu Onsen|ja|奥津温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsu Onsen|qq=}}''': Legends of imperial visits persist throughout western Japan. * '''{{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Takeo Onsen|ja|武雄温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen|qq=}}''': Linked to Empress Jingū. * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama_Onsen|'''Nanki-Shirahama Onsen''']]: Associated with Empress Jitō and other figures mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
== Legends of Discovery by Historical Figures == Discoveries attributed to historical figures often involve monks such as [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]], [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi), or [[:en:Ippen|Ippen]]. These stories reflect [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]'s emphasis on bathing as a way to dispel sickness and bring blessings, with monks often facilitating baths for the poor, ill, or prisoners.
Legends of discovery by military commanders, such as [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], are particularly common in the Tōhoku region. Many such attributions borrow names of famous figures for credibility. For instance, stories linking onsen discoveries to Kūkai often reflect the efforts of [[:en:Kōya Hijiri|Kōya Hijiri]] seeking new springs and associating them with their founder.
Legends involving prominent figures became more historically grounded during the Kamakura period, reflecting improved medical knowledge and the practical benefits of hot springs. For example: * '''Kamakura Period:''' {{ill|Ōtomo Yoriyasu|ja|大友頼泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Yoriyasu|1=Ōtomo Yoriyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433274}} established hot spring magistrates to develop {{ill|Hamawaki Onsen|ja|浜脇温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamawaki Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]. * '''Ippen:''' Stopped in Beppu in 1276, discovering {{ill|Tetsu Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsu Onsen|1=Kannawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30921762}} through a divine message from {{ill|Hi no Kami|ja|火男火売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hi no Kami|1=Honoo-Honome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566947}}.
== Legends of Discovery by Animals == Some legends credit animals with discovering onsen, often featuring "white" animals symbolizing purity or divine guidance. These include: * '''Herons:''' {{ill|Gero Onsen|ja|下呂温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gero Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Yamanaka Onsen|Yamanaka Onsen]], [[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] * '''Cranes:''' {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Foxes:''' {{ill|Yuda Onsen|ja|湯田温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda Onsen|qq=}} * '''White Wolves:''' {{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} * '''Deer:''' {{ill|Yamaga Onsen|ja|山鹿温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Kakeyu Onsen|ja|鹿教湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakeyu Onsen|qq=}} * '''Monkeys:''' {{ill|Tawarayama Onsen|ja|俵山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tawarayama Onsen|qq=}} * '''Snakes:''' {{ill|Oigami Onsen|ja|老神温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oigami Onsen|qq=}}
In reality, villagers, hunters, or woodsmen likely discovered the springs after observing animals bathing in them. The association with animals was likely used to make the story more relatable and convincing.
== References == * Noguchi, Fuyuhito. *Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Hot Springs Nationwide* (Travel Yomiuri Publishing, 1997), pp. 706–710.== See Also == * {{ill|Onsen Shrines|ja|温泉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Onsen Temple|ja|温泉寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Temple|qq=}}

== Overview == The content of these legends varies by onsen, with some having multiple legends. Not all onsen have associated legends, but they are more common among historically older hot springs.
